iPhone Photography Tips · Winter Photography

How to Shoot Stunning Winter City Photos With Your iPhone

By [Soumya Sri Gurram] · March 17, 2026 · 8 min read

Most people put their cameras away in winter. That's a mistake. Cold weather, bare trees, low light, and snow-covered cities create some of the most dramatic and beautiful photography opportunities of the entire year — and you can capture all of it with just your iPhone.

I took every single photo in this post with my iPhone, standing in Minneapolis on some of the coldest days of the year. No DSLR. No tripod. No special equipment. Just a phone, good timing, and knowing where to look.

Here's everything I learned — and exactly how you can replicate these shots in your own city.

Tip 01

Get High — Elevation Changes Everything

The single best thing you can do for winter city photography is find height. From street level, cities look flat. From above, they reveal their whole structure — the rivers, the bridges, the grid of streets, the skyline against the sky.

This shot was taken from a high-floor window looking south over the Mississippi. From street level, you'd never see this composition — the frozen Nicollet Island, the bridge arcing over the river, the city spread out behind it.

In Minneapolis, the Foshay observation deck gives you 360° views for $18. Hotel windows work just as well — book a room on a high floor and your first morning's light is worth the upgrade. Even a parking structure can give you the elevation you need.

  • Look for hotels with high-floor rooms facing the skyline
  • Foshay Tower observation deck — open daily, $18 entry
  • Parking structures and rooftop terraces are often free
  • Shoot through glass if needed — just get close to minimize reflections
Tip 02

Chase the Golden Hour — Even in Winter

Winter golden hour is short and brutal. In Minneapolis in January, you might have 25 minutes of truly magical light. But it is extraordinary when it hits. The low sun angle means light rakes across buildings, bridges, and water at angles you'll never see in summer.

The Hennepin Avenue Bridge at the last moment of golden hour. That warm amber light on the suspension towers against the deep blue sky lasted about five minutes. I pulled over immediately when I saw it.

The trick is preparation. Know exactly where the sun sets relative to your subject. Apps like PhotoPills or Sun Seeker show you the precise sun position for any date, time, and location. Set your alarm for 30 minutes before sunset and be in position. Don't rush it — be there early and let the light come to you.

"Winter golden hour lasts 20 minutes. Be in position before it starts — not after."
Tip 03

Use the Cold Sky as Your Background

One of the most underused tools in winter photography is the sky itself. Cold winter days give you this incredible deep blue that's impossible to replicate in any other season. No heat haze, no humidity — just pure, saturated blue from horizon to horizon.

Left: The downtown skyline at dusk — silhouetted against a pink-gold sky for about 4 minutes. Right: Looking north on Park Ave toward the IDS Tower, the winter trees creating a natural frame. Both taken on the same day, 90 minutes apart.

The dusk shot on the left happened by accident — I was driving somewhere else entirely. The sky turned pink and the whole skyline went on fire. I pulled over immediately. Always pull over. That's the rule.

The street shot on the right is about using what's already there. Those bare trees create a natural tunnel leading your eye straight to the IDS Tower. No editing needed — just noticing the composition that already exists.

Tip 04

Tap to Lock Exposure on Bright Light Sources

This is the most important technical tip in this entire post. When you're shooting a bright light source — a streetlight, the sun behind a building, a glowing window — tap directly on it in your iPhone camera app to lock focus and exposure there.

Your camera will darken the rest of the scene, which is exactly what you want. This is how you get that silhouette effect — the dark tree against the glowing sky, the bridge towers against the blue. Without this, your phone will try to "fix" the exposure and wash out all the drama.

Park Avenue looking north toward downtown. Notice how the foreground trees are in deep shadow while the IDS Tower catches the last of the evening light. This is exactly what exposure lock on the sky achieves.

  • Tap the bright area of your screen to expose for the highlights
  • Hold your finger down to lock AE/AF — a yellow box with a lock icon appears
  • Slide the sun icon down to reduce exposure even further for more drama
  • Shoot in RAW if your iPhone supports it for more editing flexibility
Tip 05

Let the Cold Work For You — Don't Fight It

Here's the mindset shift that changed everything for me: stop thinking of winter as an obstacle and start thinking of it as a filter. Cold weather strips cities back to their bones. The leaves are gone, so you can see structures you'd miss in summer. The crowds are thinner. The light is lower and more directional. Everything is more honest.

Dress properly — hand warmers in your pockets, gloves you can still swipe with, warm boots. Take your hands out of your pockets only to shoot. Keep your phone warm between shots to preserve battery life. And then just move slowly and look carefully.

The best winter shots aren't planned. They're noticed.
